title: Ezekiel 27
subtitle: Lament Over Tyre: The Merchant Ship

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Siege of Tyre

sections.header: Verses 1-25 — The Ship of Tyre and Its Cargo

heb.paragraph: The entire chapter personifies Tyre as a magnificent ship (oniyyah). The metaphor captures both Tyre’s grandeur and its vulnerability.
The trade catalogue in vv.12-25 is the most detailed inventory of ancient Near Eastern commerce in the Bible. Over 20 nations and their products are listed.

refs.note: Solomon’s alliance with Hiram of Tyre for temple construction.
Revelation’s list of trade goods is modelled directly on Ezekiel 27.
Isaiah’s Tyre oracle also focuses on maritime commerce.

notes.note: The ship metaphor describes Tyre’s construction from the finest international materials.
Persia, Lydia, and Libya served as Tyre’s mercenary garrison.
The trade catalogue lists over 20 trading partners: Tarshish (silver, iron), Arabia (livestock), Judah and Israel (wheat, honey, oil).
Judah and Israel trade agricultural products: wheat, confections, honey, oil, and balm.

Calvin reads the ship as an emblem of human pride: every nation contributes, yet the whole structure is vulnerable. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

Calvin: no nation is self-sufficient. True sufficiency comes only from God. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The geographic specificity is remarkable: fir from Senir, cedar from Lebanon, oaks from Bashan, cypress from Cyprus. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block calls this 'the most important single source of information on ancient Near Eastern commerce in the Hebrew Bible.'. Block's literary and theological analysis situates this passage within the author's rhetorical strategy. The text functions as both narrative and argument — the story advances the book's theological thesis about the consequences of covenant faithfulness and unfaithfulness in Israel's life under God.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli identifies two originally separate literary pieces — the ship allegory and the prose trade catalogue — combined into a single lament. Zimmerli's form-critical analysis identifies the prophetic genre and Sitz im Leben that shape this passage. The oracle's structure — introduction formula, messenger speech, motivating clause — follows established prophetic convention while deploying it with Ezekiel's characteristic visionary intensity and priestly concern.

hist.context: Chapter 27 is a remarkable literary achievement: a funeral dirge for a city portrayed as a magnificent ship. The first section describes the ship’s construction from the finest materials of the known world, and the trade catalogue reads like a customs manifest of the ancient world.

heb.paragraph: The nations 'hiss' (sharaq) at Tyre’s fall — an expression of horror and contempt. The same word describes the reaction to Jerusalem’s destruction in Jeremiah 19:8.

refs.note: God shatters the ships of Tarshish with an east wind.
The storm that wrecks Jonah’s ship uses similar imagery.
Revelation’s merchants 'stand far off' and weep directly echoing Tyre’s mourners.

notes.note: The east wind sinks the ship in the heart of the seas. Everything goes down. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

Calvin identifies the east wind as divine providence: the very elements obey God’s decree. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The mourning customs — casting dust, rolling in ashes, shaving the head — are well-attested ancient Near Eastern practices. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block notes the devastating irony: the ship metaphor that celebrated Tyre’s maritime greatness now describes its maritime destruction. Block's literary and theological analysis situates this passage within the author's rhetorical strategy. The text functions as both narrative and argument — the story advances the book's theological thesis about the consequences of covenant faithfulness and unfaithfulness in Israel's life under God.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli reads the shipwreck as a cosmic event: the collapse of an entire world economic order. Zimmerli's form-critical analysis identifies the prophetic genre and Sitz im Leben that shape this passage. The oracle's structure — introduction formula, messenger speech, motivating clause — follows established prophetic convention while deploying it with Ezekiel's characteristic visionary intensity and priestly concern.

hist.context: The magnificent ship encounters the east wind and sinks in the heart of the seas. The drowning inverts Tyre’s maritime mastery: the sea that carried her wealth becomes her grave.

themes.note: Sovereignty reaches its maximum as God sinks the ship that symbolised global trade.

lit.note: The allegory builds the magnificent ship piece by piece, loads it with the world’s wealth, then sinks it.

hebtext.note: The entire chapter personifies Tyre as a magnificent ship (oniyyah). The metaphor captures both Tyre’s grandeur and its vulnerability.
The nations 'hiss' (sharaq) at Tyre’s fall — an expression of horror and contempt. The same word describes the reaction to Jerusalem’s destruction in Jeremiah 19:8.

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: Ezekiel is a key figure in Lament Over Tyre: The Merchant Ship.

ppl.name: Daniel
ppl.text: Daniel is not a key figure in the Lament Over Tyre (Ezekiel 27). The key figure is Ezekiel as the prophet delivering the oracle.

ppl.name: Noah
ppl.text: Noah is not a figure in Ezekiel 27's Lament Over Tyre.

ppl.name: Job
ppl.text: Job is not a figure in Ezekiel 27's Lament Over Tyre.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David is not a figure in Ezekiel 27's Lament Over Tyre.

ppl.name: Moses
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Lament Over Tyre: The Merchant Ship

translations.text: Men of Sidon and Arvad were your oarsmen; your skilled men, Tyre, were aboard as your sailors.
The inhabitants of Sidon and Arvad were your rowers; your skilled men, O Tyre, were in you; they were your pilots.
Of oaks from Bashan they made your oars; of cypress wood from the coasts of Cyprus they made your deck, adorned with ivory.
Of oaks of Bashan they made your oars; they made your deck of pines from the coasts of Cyprus, inlaid with ivory.

thread.text: Solomon’s alliance with Hiram of Tyre for temple construction.
Revelation’s list of trade goods is modelled directly on Ezekiel 27.
Isaiah’s Tyre oracle also focuses on maritime commerce.
God shatters the ships of Tarshish with an east wind.
The storm that wrecks Jonah’s ship uses similar imagery.
Revelation’s merchants 'stand far off' and weep directly echoing Tyre’s mourners.

coaching.tip: The ship of Tyre is described with meticulous detail: planks from Senir, mast from Lebanon, oars from Bashan, deck from Cyprus, sails from Egypt, crew from Sidon (vv. 5–9). The inventory reads like a manifest of the entire ancient world's resources. Tyre's ship is built from the whole earth — and its sinking capsizes international commerce. Ezekiel turns a trade catalogue into a funeral program.

The nations' mourning for Tyre (vv. 29–36) is grief for lost revenue, not lost relationship. They mourn a customer, not a friend. Ezekiel captures the hollowness of commercial relationships: intense while profitable, abandoned when the balance sheet turns. Revelation 18 models its lament for 'Babylon the Great' directly on this chapter — merchants weeping not for the dead but for the market.
